trans-unit.source: Non-Spatial processing
trans-unit.target: Non-Spatial processing

trans-unit.source: Introduction
trans-unit.target: Introduction

trans-unit.source: Non-spatial data processing in QGIS refers to the manipulation of attribute data without directly involving spatial components or information, such as the spatial relationships or geometries.
trans-unit.target: Non-spatial data processing in QGIS refers to the manipulation of attribute data without directly involving spatial components or information, such as the spatial relationships or geometries.

trans-unit.source: It changes the non-geometric attributes of datasets (i.e., the attribute table).
trans-unit.target: It changes the non-geometric attributes of datasets (i.e., the attribute table).

trans-unit.source: Non-spatial processing can be used to perform calculations, generate statistics, and gain insights into the non-spatial aspects of geospatial datasets.
trans-unit.target: Non-spatial processing can be used to perform calculations, generate statistics, and gain insights into the non-spatial aspects of geospatial datasets.

trans-unit.source: QGIS offers a variety of tools for non-spatial processing to assist users in managing and analysing attribute data effectively.
trans-unit.target: QGIS offers a variety of tools for non-spatial processing to assist users in managing and analysing attribute data effectively.

trans-unit.source: This can include data cleaning, transformation, enrichment, and analysis based on the associated attribute information, such as population statistics, land use classifications, or economic indicators.
trans-unit.target: This can include data cleaning, transformation, enrichment, and analysis based on the associated attribute information, such as population statistics, land use classifications, or economic indicators.

trans-unit.source: Screenshot of an attribute table for QGIS version 3.28.4. :::
trans-unit.target: Screenshot of an attribute table for QGIS version 3.28.4. :::

trans-unit.source: Non-spatial joins (Join Attributes by Field Value)
trans-unit.target: Non-spatial joins (Join Attributes by Field Value)

trans-unit.source: A lot of analysis can be done with just a single layer. But, sometimes, the necessary information we need for our analysis is __split across__ different datasets/layers.
trans-unit.target: A lot of analysis can be done with just a single layer. But, sometimes, the necessary information we need for our analysis is __split across__ different datasets/layers.

trans-unit.source: With QGIS, these layers can be __combined__ to perform the analysis we want. The simplest way to combine layers is via an __attribute join__. This operation looks up information from a second data source based on a __shared attribute value__. This value functions as a common unique identifier, also known as an ID, UID, or key (see {numref}`simple_attr_join_example`).
trans-unit.target: With QGIS, these layers can be __combined__ to perform the analysis we want. The simplest way to combine layers is via an __attribute join__. This operation looks up information from a second data source based on a __shared attribute value__. This value functions as a common unique identifier, also known as an ID, UID, or key (see {numref}`simple_attr_join_example`).

trans-unit.source: The entries in the two data tables can be joined via the common ID-field. :::
trans-unit.target: The entries in the two data tables can be joined via the common ID-field. :::

trans-unit.source: ::::{card} __Humanitarian example:__ ^^^ *A common GIS workflow in humanitarian work involving non-spatial joins is joining data on administrative boundaries using P-codes as the common identifier/shared attribute.
trans-unit.target: ::::{card} __Humanitarian example:__ ^^^ *A common GIS workflow in humanitarian work involving non-spatial joins is joining data on administrative boundaries using P-codes as the common identifier/shared attribute.

trans-unit.source: P-codes are identifying codes for administrative units (e.g. country (adm0), region (adm1), district (adm2)), that were introduced to simplify joining tabular data on administrative regions. These codes clearly identify the administrative units facilitating non-spatial joins.
trans-unit.target: P-codes are identifying codes for administrative units (e.g. country (adm0), region (adm1), district (adm2)), that were introduced to simplify joining tabular data on administrative regions. These codes clearly identify the administrative units facilitating non-spatial joins.

trans-unit.source: For example: We have a spatial dataset containing the administrative boundaries of districts (adm2) in Nigeria and a data table containing the population per district, but without the polygons. By using the P-codes as identifying attributes, we can easily join the population data with the vector dataset.*
trans-unit.target: For example: We have a spatial dataset containing the administrative boundaries of districts (adm2) in Nigeria and a data table containing the population per district, but without the polygons. By using the P-codes as identifying attributes, we can easily join the population data with the vector dataset.*

trans-unit.source: The P-code associated with the district Edo South is NG01201. :::
trans-unit.target: The P-code associated with the district Edo South is NG01201. :::

trans-unit.source: An attribute join in QGIS only works properly, when the attributes **match exactly**.
trans-unit.target: An attribute join in QGIS only works properly, when the attributes **match exactly**.

trans-unit.source: For example: **"S. Sudan"** will not match with **"South Sudan"**.
trans-unit.target: For example: **"S. Sudan"** will not match with **"South Sudan"**.

trans-unit.source: Where possible it’s best to **use attributes that have been designed for joining**, such as **P-codes** or **ID's** which are not susceptible to spelling mistakes. :::
trans-unit.target: Where possible it’s best to **use attributes that have been designed for joining**, such as **P-codes** or **ID's** which are not susceptible to spelling mistakes. :::

trans-unit.source: Exercise: Performing a non-spatial join
trans-unit.target: Exercise: Performing a non-spatial join

trans-unit.source: In this short follow along exercise, we will add the population data to the administrative boundaries layer (adm1).
trans-unit.target: In this short follow along exercise, we will add the population data to the administrative boundaries layer (adm1).

trans-unit.source: [Download the necessary layers here](https://nexus.heigit.org/repository/gis-training-resource-center/Module_5/non_spatial_join/non_spatial_join.zip), unzip them, and add them to your QGIS-project.
trans-unit.target: [Download the necessary layers here](https://nexus.heigit.org/repository/gis-training-resource-center/Module_5/non_spatial_join/non_spatial_join.zip), unzip them, and add them to your QGIS-project.

trans-unit.source: Open the "Join Attributes by Field Value"-tool from the processing toolbox
trans-unit.target: Open the "Join Attributes by Field Value"-tool from the processing toolbox

trans-unit.source: As the Input Layer 1, select the layer `nga_admbnda_adm1_osgof_20190417`, set the "Table Field" to `ADM1_PCODE`
trans-unit.target: As the Input Layer 1, select the layer `nga_admbnda_adm1_osgof_20190417`, set the "Table Field" to `ADM1_PCODE`

trans-unit.source: As Input Layer 2, select the layer `nga_adm1pop_2022`, set the "Table Field" to `ADM1_PCODE`. Additionally, under "Layer 2 fields to copy", select `F_TL`, `M_TL`, and `T_TL`.
trans-unit.target: As Input Layer 2, select the layer `nga_adm1pop_2022`, set the "Table Field" to `ADM1_PCODE`. Additionally, under "Layer 2 fields to copy", select `F_TL`, `M_TL`, and `T_TL`.

trans-unit.source: Click `Run`. A new layer will appear in your layer panel called "Joined Layer".
trans-unit.target: Click `Run`. A new layer will appear in your layer panel called "Joined Layer".

trans-unit.source: Setting the parameters for the P-code join. :::
trans-unit.target: Setting the parameters for the P-code join. :::

trans-unit.source: Open the attribute table for the new layer and scroll to the right. Here you will find the joined attributes
trans-unit.target: Open the attribute table for the new layer and scroll to the right. Here you will find the joined attributes

trans-unit.source: Great! We have successfully added the population data to our administrative boundaries layer. Now, we can visualise the population distribution or continue to analyse our data.
trans-unit.target: Great! We have successfully added the population data to our administrative boundaries layer. Now, we can visualise the population distribution or continue to analyse our data.

trans-unit.source: The joined data is classified using the graduated symbology for the population value. :::
trans-unit.target: The joined data is classified using the graduated symbology for the population value. :::

trans-unit.source: Table functions
trans-unit.target: Table functions

trans-unit.source: Table functions usually only involve a single data layer and are manipulating the attribute table. You can add new fields, delete unwanted fields, or even calculate new fields using the __field calculator__.
trans-unit.target: Table functions usually only involve a single data layer and are manipulating the attribute table. You can add new fields, delete unwanted fields, or even calculate new fields using the __field calculator__.

trans-unit.source: For a comprehensive overview on the attribute table's functionality and its purpose, see the [Attribute table article in the wiki](content/Wiki/en_qgis_attribute_table_wiki.md).
trans-unit.target: For a comprehensive overview on the attribute table's functionality and its purpose, see the [Attribute table article in the wiki](content/Wiki/en_qgis_attribute_table_wiki.md).

trans-unit.source: Add field
trans-unit.target: Add field

trans-unit.source: The information within a vector layer can be accessed through its __attribute table__, and it can be enhanced by __introducing new fields__ to this table. These additional fields may be derived from calculations, as exemplified in the following case, where population density is computed to provide deeper insights into spatial population distributions.
trans-unit.target: The information within a vector layer can be accessed through its __attribute table__, and it can be enhanced by __introducing new fields__ to this table. These additional fields may be derived from calculations, as exemplified in the following case, where population density is computed to provide deeper insights into spatial population distributions.

trans-unit.source: :::{Attention} The selection of the appropriate data type should align with the information being added to the new attribute field. Please keep this in mind while watching the example video. ::: __Possible data types:__
trans-unit.target: :::{Attention} The selection of the appropriate data type should align with the information being added to the new attribute field. Please keep this in mind while watching the example video. ::: __Possible data types:__

trans-unit.source: The most common ones are:
trans-unit.target: The most common ones are:

trans-unit.source: __Whole number: Integer (32 and 64 bit)__
trans-unit.target: __Whole number: Integer (32 and 64 bit)__

trans-unit.source: __Decimal number (real)__
trans-unit.target: __Decimal number (real)__

trans-unit.source: __Text (string)__
trans-unit.target: __Text (string)__

trans-unit.source: Additional options:
trans-unit.target: Additional options:

trans-unit.source: Date and Date and time
trans-unit.target: Date and Date and time

trans-unit.source: Boolean
trans-unit.target: Boolean

trans-unit.source:
:::{dropdown} Example: Add a field for population density
:::
trans-unit.target:
:::{dropdown} Example: Add a field for population density
:::

trans-unit.source: Delete field
trans-unit.target: Delete field

trans-unit.source: It is also possible to __delete fields__ from the attribute table. A commonly used practice is to __remove all unused or unnecessary fields__ from a layer before starting to work on it. This __makes the dataset much more organised__.
trans-unit.target: It is also possible to __delete fields__ from the attribute table. A commonly used practice is to __remove all unused or unnecessary fields__ from a layer before starting to work on it. This __makes the dataset much more organised__.

trans-unit.source:
:::{dropdown} Example: Delete all unused/unnecessary fields from a vector layer
:::
trans-unit.target:
:::{dropdown} Example: Delete all unused/unnecessary fields from a vector layer
:::

trans-unit.source: Calculate field
trans-unit.target: Calculate field

trans-unit.source: An important practice is to calculate the attribute values for a field (e.g., based on the values of other fields). In QGIS, you can __create a new field or update an existing field__.
trans-unit.target: An important practice is to calculate the attribute values for a field (e.g., based on the values of other fields). In QGIS, you can __create a new field or update an existing field__.

trans-unit.source: It is necessary to __check if the data type of the field__ (new or updated) __and your calculation match__. For example, if you are calculating a ratio (e.g. density), the field should not be of type integer but rather of type decimal number. :::
trans-unit.target: It is necessary to __check if the data type of the field__ (new or updated) __and your calculation match__. For example, if you are calculating a ratio (e.g. density), the field should not be of type integer but rather of type decimal number. :::

trans-unit.source: An example could be to calculate the population density based on the already existing fields Population and Area.
trans-unit.target: An example could be to calculate the population density based on the already existing fields Population and Area.

trans-unit.source: A very important tool for such calculations is the __Field Calculator__. It allows you to __perform calculations based on existing attribute values or defined functions__, for example, to calculate the length or area of a geometry feature or in the given example, could be used to calculate the population density based on the already existing fields Population and Area. The results of these calculations can be written into a new field or update an existing field.
trans-unit.target: A very important tool for such calculations is the __Field Calculator__. It allows you to __perform calculations based on existing attribute values or defined functions__, for example, to calculate the length or area of a geometry feature or in the given example, could be used to calculate the population density based on the already existing fields Population and Area. The results of these calculations can be written into a new field or update an existing field.

trans-unit.source: Screenshot of the Field calculator in QGIS 3.36. :::
trans-unit.target: Screenshot of the Field calculator in QGIS 3.36. :::

trans-unit.source: The most important groups and their respective functionality that are provided with the field calculator are listed below:
trans-unit.target: The most important groups and their respective functionality that are provided with the field calculator are listed below:

trans-unit.source: __Fields and Values__
trans-unit.target: __Fields and Values__

trans-unit.source: Contains a list of fields from the layer
trans-unit.target: Contains a list of fields from the layer

trans-unit.source: __Geometry__
trans-unit.target: __Geometry__

trans-unit.source: Calculates the area of a polygon feature: `$area`
trans-unit.target: Calculates the area of a polygon feature: `$area`

trans-unit.source: Calculates the length of a line feature: `$length`
trans-unit.target: Calculates the length of a line feature: `$length`

trans-unit.source: Calculates the centroid of a polygon feature: `centroid($geometry)`
trans-unit.target: Calculates the centroid of a polygon feature: `centroid($geometry)`

trans-unit.source: Calculates the bounding box of a feature: `bounds($geometry)`
trans-unit.target: Calculates the bounding box of a feature: `bounds($geometry)`

trans-unit.source: Calculates the distance between two points: `distance(point_a, point_b)`
trans-unit.target: Calculates the distance between two points: `distance(point_a, point_b)`

trans-unit.source: __Maths__
trans-unit.target: __Maths__

trans-unit.source: Calculates the square root of a field: `sqrt("field")`
trans-unit.target: Calculates the square root of a field: `sqrt("field")`

trans-unit.source: Calculate `min` and `max`
trans-unit.target: Calculate `min` and `max`

trans-unit.source:
:::{dropdown} Example: Calculate the population density
:::
trans-unit.target:
:::{dropdown} Example: Calculate the population density
:::

trans-unit.source: Basic statistics for fields
trans-unit.target: Basic statistics for fields

trans-unit.source: The tool __Basic statistics for fields__ generates statistics for a specific field of the attribute table of a vector layer. The results are generated as an HTML file and can be accessed by using the __file path link__ in the __Results Viewer__. This operation is highly valuable for gaining a comprehensive understanding of the data you intend to work with. It allows you to determine the range of values, pinpoint the minimum and maximum values. In the provided example, this operation is applied to calculate the global population density, allowing you to easily identify the most densely populated region worldwide.
trans-unit.target: The tool __Basic statistics for fields__ generates statistics for a specific field of the attribute table of a vector layer. The results are generated as an HTML file and can be accessed by using the __file path link__ in the __Results Viewer__. This operation is highly valuable for gaining a comprehensive understanding of the data you intend to work with. It allows you to determine the range of values, pinpoint the minimum and maximum values. In the provided example, this operation is applied to calculate the global population density, allowing you to easily identify the most densely populated region worldwide.

trans-unit.source:
:::{dropdown} Example: Calculate statistics for the field population density for countries worldwide.
:::
trans-unit.target:
:::{dropdown} Example: Calculate statistics for the field population density for countries worldwide.
:::

trans-unit.source: Statistics by categories
trans-unit.target: Statistics by categories

trans-unit.source: To calculate statistics of a field depending on a parent class you can use the tool __Statistics by categories__. The parent class is a combination of values from other fields.
trans-unit.target: To calculate statistics of a field depending on a parent class you can use the tool __Statistics by categories__. The parent class is a combination of values from other fields.

trans-unit.source: __Questions that need to be considered when doing these calculations:__
trans-unit.target: __Questions that need to be considered when doing these calculations:__

trans-unit.source: For which fields should the statistics be calculated in the attribute table?
trans-unit.target: For which fields should the statistics be calculated in the attribute table?

trans-unit.source: Which field in the attribute table contains which information?
trans-unit.target: Which field in the attribute table contains which information?

trans-unit.source: For greater precision in these calculations, "statistics by categories" offers more comprehensive insights than those mentioned earlier. In this case, it becomes simple to determine the number of cities per country with over 300,000 inhabitants and, for each country, the population living in the largest urban agglomeration.
trans-unit.target: For greater precision in these calculations, "statistics by categories" offers more comprehensive insights than those mentioned earlier. In this case, it becomes simple to determine the number of cities per country with over 300,000 inhabitants and, for each country, the population living in the largest urban agglomeration.

trans-unit.source:
:::{dropdown} Example: Cities with more than 300,000 inhabitants and the amount of population in the largest agglomerations
:::
trans-unit.target:
:::{dropdown} Example: Cities with more than 300,000 inhabitants and the amount of population in the largest agglomerations
:::

trans-unit.source: Non-spatial queries
trans-unit.target: Non-spatial queries

trans-unit.source: In GIS, you can __query__ (filter) data based on specific attribute information. Once the filtering is successful, only the desired features that __correspond__ to the chosen attribute are displayed. Data filtering is a valuable technique for creating __subsets__ of features that can be exported as a new layer.
trans-unit.target: In GIS, you can __query__ (filter) data based on specific attribute information. Once the filtering is successful, only the desired features that __correspond__ to the chosen attribute are displayed. Data filtering is a valuable technique for creating __subsets__ of features that can be exported as a new layer.

trans-unit.source: Manual selection
trans-unit.target: Manual selection

trans-unit.source: It is possible to manually select specific rows by clicking on the number on the left side of it. This can be easily used to select a small number of rows. If they are selected successfully, they will appear in __yellow__.
trans-unit.target: It is possible to manually select specific rows by clicking on the number on the left side of it. This can be easily used to select a small number of rows. If they are selected successfully, they will appear in __yellow__.

trans-unit.source:
:::{dropdown} Example: Manual selection of rows
:::
trans-unit.target:
:::{dropdown} Example: Manual selection of rows
:::

trans-unit.source: Select by expression
trans-unit.target: Select by expression

trans-unit.source: In this dialog, you can build your expressions to query the data. There are several operators that can be used to filter your vector layer.
trans-unit.target: In this dialog, you can build your expressions to query the data. There are several operators that can be used to filter your vector layer.

trans-unit.source: :::{tab-item} Arithmetic operators | operator | functionality | |----------|------------------------| | `+` | addition | | `-` | subtraction | | `*` | multiplication | | `/` | division | | `%` | remainder of division | :::
trans-unit.target: :::{tab-item} Arithmetic operators | operator | functionality | |----------|------------------------| | `+` | addition | | `-` | subtraction | | `*` | multiplication | | `/` | division | | `%` | remainder of division | :::

trans-unit.source: :::{tab-item} Comparison operators | operator | functionality | |----------|--------------------------| | `=` | equals | | `!=` | not equal | | `<` | less than | | `>` | greater than | | `<=` | less than or equal to | | `>=` | greater than or equal to | :::
trans-unit.target: :::{tab-item} Comparison operators | operator | functionality | |----------|--------------------------| | `=` | equals | | `!=` | not equal | | `<` | less than | | `>` | greater than | | `<=` | less than or equal to | | `>=` | greater than or equal to | :::

trans-unit.source: :::{tab-item} Logical operators Operators such as AND, OR can be used to combine different queries or criteria | operator | functionality | |----------|------------------------| | `AND` | logical AND | | `OR` | logical OR | | `NOT` | logical NOT | :::
trans-unit.target: :::{tab-item} Logical operators Operators such as AND, OR can be used to combine different queries or criteria | operator | functionality | |----------|------------------------| | `AND` | logical AND | | `OR` | logical OR | | `NOT` | logical NOT | :::

trans-unit.source: :::{tab-item} Special operators | operator | functionality | |---------------|------------------------------------------------| | `LIKE` | pattern matching | | `IN` | checks if a value is in a list of values | | `IS NULL` | checks for null values | | `BETWEEN` | checks if a value is within a specified range | | `CASE WHEN` | conditional expressions | :::
trans-unit.target: :::{tab-item} Special operators | operator | functionality | |---------------|------------------------------------------------| | `LIKE` | pattern matching | | `IN` | checks if a value is in a list of values | | `IS NULL` | checks for null values | | `BETWEEN` | checks if a value is within a specified range | | `CASE WHEN` | conditional expressions | :::

trans-unit.source: Querying your data to answer more complex questions is of great importance. This can be accomplished using the "Select by expression" tool. In the provided example, we aim to answer the question: Which cities, excluding those with a population of one million inhabitants in 1950, had grown to over ten million inhabitants by 2015?
trans-unit.target: Querying your data to answer more complex questions is of great importance. This can be accomplished using the "Select by expression" tool. In the provided example, we aim to answer the question: Which cities, excluding those with a population of one million inhabitants in 1950, had grown to over ten million inhabitants by 2015?

trans-unit.source:
:::{dropdown} Example: Cities, excluding those with a population of one million inhabitants in 1950, that have grown to over ten million inhabitants by 2015
:::
trans-unit.target:
:::{dropdown} Example: Cities, excluding those with a population of one million inhabitants in 1950, that have grown to over ten million inhabitants by 2015
:::

trans-unit.source: SQL
trans-unit.target: SQL

trans-unit.source: Another possibility to build your expressions is to use SQL.
trans-unit.target: Another possibility to build your expressions is to use SQL.

trans-unit.source: :::{tab-item} SQL introduction SQL (Structured Query Language) is a standardised programming language that is used to manage databases and perform various operations on the data in them. In the Query Builder in QGIS, you can use SQL expressions to use one or more conditions to filter a layer. :::
trans-unit.target: :::{tab-item} SQL introduction SQL (Structured Query Language) is a standardised programming language that is used to manage databases and perform various operations on the data in them. In the Query Builder in QGIS, you can use SQL expressions to use one or more conditions to filter a layer. :::

trans-unit.source: :::{tab-item} SQL Cheat Sheet You can easily access essential SQL statements by referring to this handy [Cheat Sheet](https://www.sqltutorial.org/sql-cheat-sheet/). This offers a concise overview of the core functionalities. :::
trans-unit.target: :::{tab-item} SQL Cheat Sheet You can easily access essential SQL statements by referring to this handy [Cheat Sheet](https://www.sqltutorial.org/sql-cheat-sheet/). This offers a concise overview of the core functionalities. :::

trans-unit.source: Query Builder
trans-unit.target: Query Builder

trans-unit.source: The Query Builder provides an interface that allows you to define a __subset of the features__ in the layer using SQL-like statements and to display the results in the main window. As long as the query is active, only the __features corresponding__ to its result are available in the project. You can use one or more layer attributes to define the filter in the Query Builder. The Query Builder is built as follows:
trans-unit.target: The Query Builder provides an interface that allows you to define a __subset of the features__ in the layer using SQL-like statements and to display the results in the main window. As long as the query is active, only the __features corresponding__ to its result are available in the project. You can use one or more layer attributes to define the filter in the Query Builder. The Query Builder is built as follows:

trans-unit.source: Screenshot of the Query Builder. :::
trans-unit.target: Screenshot of the Query Builder. :::

trans-unit.source: The __Fields list__ contains all the fields of the layer. To add an attribute column to the expression window, double-click its name or just type it into the box.
trans-unit.target: The __Fields list__ contains all the fields of the layer. To add an attribute column to the expression window, double-click its name or just type it into the box.

trans-unit.source: The __Values__ frame lists the values of the currently selected field.
trans-unit.target: The __Values__ frame lists the values of the currently selected field.

trans-unit.source: To list __all unique values__ of a field, click the __All__ button.
trans-unit.target: To list __all unique values__ of a field, click the __All__ button.

trans-unit.source: To list the __first 25__ unique values of the column, click the __Sample__ button.
trans-unit.target: To list the __first 25__ unique values of the column, click the __Sample__ button.

trans-unit.source: To add a value to the expression window, double click it in the Values list. You can use the __search box__ at the top of the "Values"-panel to easily browse and find attribute values in the list.
trans-unit.target: To add a value to the expression window, double click it in the Values list. You can use the __search box__ at the top of the "Values"-panel to easily browse and find attribute values in the list.

trans-unit.source: The __Operators__ section contains all usable operators. To add an operator, click the appropriate button.
trans-unit.target: The __Operators__ section contains all usable operators. To add an operator, click the appropriate button.

trans-unit.source: The __Test__ button helps you to check your query and __displays a message box with the number of features__ satisfying the current query.
trans-unit.target: The __Test__ button helps you to check your query and __displays a message box with the number of features__ satisfying the current query.

trans-unit.source: Use the __Clear__ button to revert the layer to its original state.
trans-unit.target: Use the __Clear__ button to revert the layer to its original state.

trans-unit.source: In this short video, you will discover the location of the query builder and learn how to create a straightforward query for isolating a particular state from a dataset that covers the entire country. The example focuses on a dataset related to South Sudan and serves as a basic illustration.
trans-unit.target: In this short video, you will discover the location of the query builder and learn how to create a straightforward query for isolating a particular state from a dataset that covers the entire country. The example focuses on a dataset related to South Sudan and serves as a basic illustration.

trans-unit.source:
:::{dropdown} Example: Simple usage of the Query Builder.
:::
trans-unit.target:
:::{dropdown} Example: Simple usage of the Query Builder.
:::

trans-unit.source: Self-Assessment Questions
trans-unit.target: Self-Assessment Questions

trans-unit.source: ::::{admonition} Test your knowledge :class: note
trans-unit.target: ::::{admonition} Test your knowledge :class: note

trans-unit.source: __What does “non‑spatial processing” mean in the context of GIS/QGIS? How does it differ from spatial operations?__
trans-unit.target: __What does “non‑spatial processing” mean in the context of GIS/QGIS? How does it differ from spatial operations?__

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Answer
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Answer

trans-unit.source: Non‑spatial processing refers to operations that act solely on the attribute side (the table or data columns) of a layer, without using or modifying the geometry (location, shape) of features. You are manipulating data about features, not their spatial relationships.
trans-unit.target: Non‑spatial processing refers to operations that act solely on the attribute side (the table or data columns) of a layer, without using or modifying the geometry (location, shape) of features. You are manipulating data about features, not their spatial relationships.

trans-unit.source: Examples include adding, deleting, or updating fields; filtering records based on attribute queries; performing table joins based on matching key fields; computing new values from existing attributes.
trans-unit.target: Examples include adding, deleting, or updating fields; filtering records based on attribute queries; performing table joins based on matching key fields; computing new values from existing attributes.

trans-unit.source: In contrast, spatial operations involve geometry — they use spatial relationships (proximity, intersection, containment) and often produce changes in the shape, size, or arrangement of features (for example buffer, intersect, clip, union). Spatial tools compute new geometry or restructure existing geometry. :::
trans-unit.target: In contrast, spatial operations involve geometry — they use spatial relationships (proximity, intersection, containment) and often produce changes in the shape, size, or arrangement of features (for example buffer, intersect, clip, union). Spatial tools compute new geometry or restructure existing geometry. :::

trans-unit.source: __What is a “non‑spatial join” (Join Attributes by Field Value)? Describe how and when you would use it.__
trans-unit.target: __What is a “non‑spatial join” (Join Attributes by Field Value)? Describe how and when you would use it.__

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Answer
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Answer

trans-unit.source: A non‑spatial join (in QGIS often called Join Attributes by Field Value) links attribute data from one table (or non‑spatial data) to another feature layer based on a common key (field) value, not based on geometry. It is also called a tabular join or attribute join.
trans-unit.target: A non‑spatial join (in QGIS often called Join Attributes by Field Value) links attribute data from one table (or non‑spatial data) to another feature layer based on a common key (field) value, not based on geometry. It is also called a tabular join or attribute join.

trans-unit.source: You'd use it when you have two datasets that share a common attribute (e.g. a region code, an ID, a name) and you want to enrich one layer with additional attributes from the other. For example: you have a layer of administrative polygons and a separate table of population statistics keyed by the same admin code; you want to bring those population figures into the polygon layer so you can map or analyze them.
trans-unit.target: You'd use it when you have two datasets that share a common attribute (e.g. a region code, an ID, a name) and you want to enrich one layer with additional attributes from the other. For example: you have a layer of administrative polygons and a separate table of population statistics keyed by the same admin code; you want to bring those population figures into the polygon layer so you can map or analyze them.

trans-unit.source: The non-spatial join adds fields from one layer or dataset into the target layer’s attribute table where the key matches.
trans-unit.target: The non-spatial join adds fields from one layer or dataset into the target layer’s attribute table where the key matches.

trans-unit.source: __What conditions must be true for a non‑spatial join to work correctly? (E.g. key fields, matching values, data types)__
trans-unit.target: __What conditions must be true for a non‑spatial join to work correctly? (E.g. key fields, matching values, data types)__

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Answer
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Answer

trans-unit.source: __Common key field present in both tables__
trans-unit.target: __Common key field present in both tables__

trans-unit.source: Both the target layer (the one receiving the join) and the join table must have a field (column) that is the “join key,” which contains matching identifiers in corresponding rows.
trans-unit.target: Both the target layer (the one receiving the join) and the join table must have a field (column) that is the “join key,” which contains matching identifiers in corresponding rows.

trans-unit.source: __Matching values in those key fields__
trans-unit.target: __Matching values in those key fields__

trans-unit.source: The values in the key field must overlap: for each feature in the target layer that you want to get joined data for, there must be a matching value in the join table’s key field. If there is no match, the joined value will typically be NULL or blank.
trans-unit.target: The values in the key field must overlap: for each feature in the target layer that you want to get joined data for, there must be a matching value in the join table’s key field. If there is no match, the joined value will typically be NULL or blank.

trans-unit.source: __Compatible data types__
trans-unit.target: __Compatible data types__

trans-unit.source: The key fields should be of the same or compatible data type (e.g. both integer, or both text) so that QGIS can match them. If one is a text field and the other is numeric, matching won’t work reliably.
trans-unit.target: The key fields should be of the same or compatible data type (e.g. both integer, or both text) so that QGIS can match them. If one is a text field and the other is numeric, matching won’t work reliably.

trans-unit.source: __Uniqueness of values__
trans-unit.target: __Uniqueness of values__

trans-unit.source: Ideally, the join table should have unique values in the join key (one-to-one join). If there are multiple matching rows (one-to-many), then you might end up with duplicates, or ambiguous matching — QGIS may either take the first match or aggregate, depending on the join method.
trans-unit.target: Ideally, the join table should have unique values in the join key (one-to-one join). If there are multiple matching rows (one-to-many), then you might end up with duplicates, or ambiguous matching — QGIS may either take the first match or aggregate, depending on the join method.

trans-unit.source: The target layer normally does not change its number of features — the join just appends fields, not duplicating geometry (unless you convert to a permanent joined file in a different way).
trans-unit.target: The target layer normally does not change its number of features — the join just appends fields, not duplicating geometry (unless you convert to a permanent joined file in a different way).

trans-unit.source: __What kinds of operations can you perform using table functions (add field, delete field, calculate field)?__
trans-unit.target: __What kinds of operations can you perform using table functions (add field, delete field, calculate field)?__

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Answer
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Answer

trans-unit.source: __Add field__: Create a new attribute column (of a chosen type, e.g. integer, decimal, text) to hold computed or imported values.
trans-unit.target: __Add field__: Create a new attribute column (of a chosen type, e.g. integer, decimal, text) to hold computed or imported values.

trans-unit.source: __Delete field__: Remove unwanted or redundant fields/columns from a layer’s attribute table.
trans-unit.target: __Delete field__: Remove unwanted or redundant fields/columns from a layer’s attribute table.

trans-unit.source: __Calculate field (Field Calculator)__: Compute new values (for existing or new fields) using expressions (arithmetic, conditional logic, functions) based on other attributes, constants, or functions. For example: calculate population density = population / area; or classify values using CASE WHEN logic.
trans-unit.target: __Calculate field (Field Calculator)__: Compute new values (for existing or new fields) using expressions (arithmetic, conditional logic, functions) based on other attributes, constants, or functions. For example: calculate population density = population / area; or classify values using CASE WHEN logic.

trans-unit.source: __Update/Edit existing field__: You can also update or overwrite values in an existing field, provided the layer is in edit mode. :::
trans-unit.target: __Update/Edit existing field__: You can also update or overwrite values in an existing field, provided the layer is in edit mode. :::

trans-unit.source: __What is a non‑spatial query? How would you use “Select by expression” to filter your data?__
trans-unit.target: __What is a non‑spatial query? How would you use “Select by expression” to filter your data?__

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Answer
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Answer

trans-unit.source: A non‑spatial query is a query applied to the attribute table (fields) rather than based on geometry. It filters or selects rows (features) based on attribute conditions, not spatial conditions.
trans-unit.target: A non‑spatial query is a query applied to the attribute table (fields) rather than based on geometry. It filters or selects rows (features) based on attribute conditions, not spatial conditions.

trans-unit.source: In QGIS, “Select by expression” is a tool you can use to write logical expressions (e.g. `"population" > 10000 AND "region" = 'East'`) to select the subset of features whose attributes satisfy the criteria.
trans-unit.target: In QGIS, “Select by expression” is a tool you can use to write logical expressions (e.g. `"population" > 10000 AND "region" = 'East'`) to select the subset of features whose attributes satisfy the criteria.

trans-unit.source: After selection, you can use the selected subset for further operations (e.g. export selected features, style them differently, analyze only them, etc.).
trans-unit.target: After selection, you can use the selected subset for further operations (e.g. export selected features, style them differently, analyze only them, etc.).
